papers.title: Aligning Attention with Human Rationales for Self-Explaining Hate Speech Detection
papers.notes: DIRECT CONCURRENT WORK. Uses layer 8 head 7 of BERT, MSE loss, softmax attention. No head selection, no sparsemax, <=3 seeds. 31% annotation inconsistencies in HateXplain test set.

papers.title: Self-Explaining Hate Speech Detection with Moral Rationales
papers.notes: DIRECT CONCURRENT WORK. Extends SRA with moral rationales (Moral Foundations Theory). Introduces HateBRMoralXplain. Same mechanistic limitations as SRA. Portuguese-centric.

papers.title: HateXplain: A Benchmark Dataset for Explainable Hate Speech Detection
papers.notes: Primary evaluation dataset. 20,148 posts, 3-class (hate/offensive/normal), 3 annotators, token-level rationales. LIME-based plausibility/faithfulness evaluation. Variable inter-annotator agreement.

papers.title: Attention is not Explanation
papers.notes: Seminal paper showing attention is not faithful by default. Motivates supervised attention approach. ~1000+ citations.

papers.title: Attention is not not Explanation
papers.notes: Rebuttal to Jain & Wallace. Attention can constitute explanation under conditions. Provides formal faithfulness tests.

papers.title: From Softmax to Sparsemax: A Sparse Model of Attention and Multi-Label Classification
papers.notes: Sparsemax activation function. Sparse attention output. Comparable to softmax in NMT/NLI. Core technical component.

papers.title: Adaptively Sparse Transformers
papers.notes: α-entmax with learnable per-head α. Heads learn different sparsity preferences. Improves interpretability and head diversity in NMT.

papers.title: ERASER: A Benchmark to Evaluate Rationalized NLP Models
papers.notes: 762 citations. Standard benchmark for rationale evaluation. Defines comprehensiveness and sufficiency metrics we use.

papers.title: What Does BERT Look at? An Analysis of BERT's Attention
papers.notes: BERT head specialization: syntactic, positional, delimiter heads. Motivates selective head supervision.

papers.title: Analyzing Multi-Head Self-Attention: Specialized Heads Do the Heavy Lifting, the Rest Can Be Pruned
papers.notes: Differentiable L0 head pruning. ~80% heads prunable. Specialized heads (positional, syntactic, rare word) last to be pruned. Template for head importance.

papers.title: Are Sixteen Heads Really Better than One?
papers.notes: Gradient-based head importance: I(h,l) = E_x[|dL/dA^h,l|]. Most heads prunable at test time. Provides importance formula for our head selection.

papers.title: Axiomatic Attribution for Deep Networks
papers.notes: Integrated Gradients. Satisfies Sensitivity and Implementation Invariance. Primary faithfulness evaluator replacing LIME in our work.

papers.title: Why Is It Hate Speech? Masked Rationale Prediction for Explainable Hate Speech Detection
papers.notes: Masked Rationale Prediction intermediate task. Baseline for our work, stronger than vanilla BERT on HateXplain. Pre-SRA SOTA.

papers.title: Sparse Sequence-to-Sequence Models
papers.notes: α-entmax family for NMT. Sparse alignments and output distributions. Provides technical basis for Correia et al. 2019.

papers.title: Towards Interpretable Hate Speech Detection using Large Language Model-extracted Rationales
papers.notes: LLM-extracted rationales for interpretable hate speech classification. Alternative to human annotation. Baseline paradigm.

papers.title: HateBRXplain: A Benchmark Dataset with Human-Annotated Rationales for Explainable Hate Speech Detection in Brazilian Portuguese
papers.notes: 7,000 Brazilian Portuguese Instagram comments with expert rationale annotations. 9 hate targets. Used by SRA for cross-lingual evaluation.

papers.title: Towards Efficient and Explainable Hate Speech Detection via Model Distillation
papers.notes: Chain-of-thought distillation from LLMs for hate speech. Distilled small models explain + classify. Strong classification baseline.

papers.title: D3CODE: Disentangling Disagreements in Data across Cultures on Offensiveness Detection and Evaluation
papers.notes: Cross-cultural offensiveness dataset. Moral values explain annotator disagreement more than demographics. Motivates agreement stratification.

papers.title: Hate Speech Detection via Dual Contrastive Learning
papers.notes: Dual contrastive learning; focal loss for imbalance. Captures span-level information. Useful comparison baseline.

papers.title: Pruning and Sparsemax Methods for Hierarchical Attention Networks
papers.notes: Sparsemax in hierarchical attention for document classification. Limited gains on IMDB. No rationale supervision.

papers.title: Can Rationalization Improve Robustness?
papers.notes: Rationale models for adversarial robustness. Human rationales don't always improve robustness. Useful negative result.

papers.title: Measuring Association Between Labels and Free-Text Rationales
papers.notes: Label-rationale association as faithfulness necessity. Robustness equivalence and feature importance agreement tests.

papers.title: Right for the Right Reasons: Training Differentiable Models by Constraining their Explanations
papers.cross_field_note: Foundational EGL paper. Constrains input gradients to match human annotation masks. Analogous to our attention alignment but operates in gradient space, not attention space. Must cite in related work as prior art in the EGL field.

papers.title: Hybrid Explanation-Guided Learning for Transformer-Based Chest X-Ray Diagnosis
papers.cross_field_note: Hybrid self-supervised + human-guided attention alignment for Vision Transformers in medical imaging. Independent parallel motivation for EGL in transformers. No head selection, no sparsemax, different domain.

papers.title: Concept Bottleneck Models
papers.cross_field_note: Intermediate concept supervision: model predicts human-labeled concepts before final output. Related in spirit (intermediate supervision) but different mechanism (concept bottleneck vs. attention head alignment).

papers.title: SPLADE: Sparse Lexical and Expansion Model for First Stage Ranking
papers.cross_field_note: Sparse token-weight representations from BERT for IR. Uses ReLU+log-saturation for sparsity, not sparsemax. Different task (retrieval vs classification), different supervision (relevance labels vs rationale masks).

papers.title: Aligning Human and Machine Attention for Enhanced Supervised Learning
papers.cross_field_note: Human-Machine Attention Learning: aligns BERT/GPT-2 attention with self-reported human attention for sentiment analysis. No head selection, no sparsemax, no hate speech domain. Different supervision signal (self-reported vs expert rationale annotations).

papers.title: Improving the Faithfulness of Attention-based Explanations with Task-specific Information for Text Classification
papers.notes: Task-Scaling (TaSc): learns task-specific scaling factors to modulate attention weights for improved faithfulness. Tested on 5 datasets, 5 encoders. No rationale supervision, no sparsemax, no hate speech. Different mechanism: scale existing weights vs. replace activation and add supervision.

papers.title: The Explanation Game: Towards Prediction Explainability through Sparse Communication
papers.notes: Uses α-entmax (sparsemax family) for sparse communication-based explanation. Unsupervised sparse token selection — no human rationale annotations, no hate speech, no head selection. Key differential: unsupervised vs. supervised sparse alignment.

papers.title: Learning to Faithfully Rationalize by Construction
papers.notes: FRESH: extractor+predictor pipeline for faithful rationalization by construction. Predictor sees only extracted tokens. Different mechanism from attention alignment — structural faithfulness (input masking) vs. distributional faithfulness (attention alignment). Background.

papers.title: Human Attention Maps for Text Classification: Do Humans and Neural Networks Focus on the Same Words?
papers.notes: Compares human (crowdsourced) attention with neural attention in text classification. Finds misalignment. Motivation for rationale supervision, not a concurrent method.

papers.title: Do Human Rationales Improve Machine Explanations?
papers.notes: Studies whether human rationale supervision improves model-generated explanations (extractive rationale tasks). Positive finding motivates our approach. Background.

papers.title: X-MuTeST: A Multilingual Benchmark for Explainable Hate Speech Detection and A Novel LLM-consulted Explanation Framework
papers.notes: Multilingual benchmark for explainable hate speech (Hindi, Telugu, English). English uses HateXplain rationales (6,334 samples). Combines LLM explanations with token probability-difference scores to refine model attention. Evaluates IoU-F1, Token-F1, Comprehensiveness, Sufficiency. No BERT attention supervision, no sparsemax, no alignment loss.

papers.title: Bi-Attention HateXplain: Taking into account the sequential aspect of data during explainability in a multi-task context
papers.notes: BiRNN + bidirectional attention multi-task model for HateXplain explainability + classification. Addresses attention variability in prior work. No BERT, no sparsemax, no rationale alignment loss, no head selection.

papers.title: Explainable AI: Context-Aware Layer-Wise Integrated Gradients for Explaining Transformer Models
papers.notes: Layer-wise IG attribution framework for Transformers; applied to hate speech detection (XLM-R, AfroLM). Independent confirmation that IG is superior to LIME for hate speech explainability. Strengthens H4 motivation. Post-hoc attribution only, no training-time modification.
